section.title: Seventy Years of Captivity
section.sectionRationale: This chapter is a hinge for the whole book: it looks back over twenty-three years of ignored warnings, fixes the coming exile at a specific length, seventy years, and then widens the lens from Judah alone to every nation, who must all drink the same cup of God's wrath from His hand.
section.thesis: Twenty-three years of patient warning have run out; Babylon's rule and Judah's exile are now fixed at seventy years, and that same cup of wrath, first pressed to Judah's lips, must be drunk by every nation on earth, for the LORD is bringing judgment on all mankind.
section.frameNote: Four movements: a summary of twenty-three years of unheeded prophetic warning, the seventy years of captivity decreed, the cup of wrath passed to every surrounding nation, and the LORD's roar of judgment against the whole earth.

verses.text: This is the word that came to Jeremiah concerning all the people of Judah in the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah king of Judah, which was the first year of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon.
So the prophet Jeremiah spoke to all the people of Judah and all the residents of Jerusalem as follows:

“From the thirteenth year of Josiah son of Amon king of Judah until this very day, twenty-three years, the word of the LORD has come to me, and I have spoken to you again and again, but you have not listened.
And the LORD has sent all His servants the prophets to you again and again, but you have not listened or inclined your ear to hear.
The prophets told you, ‘Turn now, each of you, from your evil ways and deeds, and you can dwell in the land that the LORD has given to you and your fathers forever and ever.
Do not follow other gods to serve and worship them, and do not provoke Me to anger with the works of your hands. Then I will do you no harm.’
‘But to your own harm, you have not listened to Me,’ declares the LORD, ‘so you have provoked Me to anger with the works of your hands.’

units.label: Seventy years decreed
units.note: a fixed length to the discipline

verses.text: Therefore this is what the LORD of Hosts says: ‘Because you have not obeyed My words,

behold, I will summon all the families of the north, declares the LORD, and I will send for My servant Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, whom I will bring against this land, against its residents, and against all the surrounding nations. So I will devote them to destruction and make them an object of horror and contempt, an everlasting desolation.
Moreover, I will banish from them the sounds of joy and gladness, the voices of the bride and bridegroom, the sound of the millstones, and the light of the lamp.
And this whole land will become a desolate wasteland, and these nations will serve the king of Babylon for {{shivim|seventy years}}.
But when seventy years are complete, I will punish the king of Babylon and that nation, the land of the Chaldeans, for their guilt, declares the LORD, and I will make it an everlasting desolation.
I will bring upon that land all the words I have pronounced against it, all that is written in this book, which Jeremiah has prophesied against all the nations.
For many nations and great kings will enslave them, and I will repay them according to their deeds and according to the work of their hands.’”

units.label: The cup of wrath for all nations

verses.text: This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, said to me: “Take from My hand this {{kos|cup}} of the wine of wrath, and make all the nations to whom I send you drink from it.
And they will drink and stagger and go out of their minds, because of the sword that I will send among them.”
So I took the cup from the LORD's hand and made all the nations drink from it, each one to whom the LORD had sent me,
to make them a ruin, an object of horror and contempt and cursing, as they are to this day, Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, its kings and officials;
Pharaoh king of Egypt, his officials, his leaders, and all his people;
all the mixed tribes; all the kings of Uz; all the kings of the Philistines: Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron, and the remnant of Ashdod;
Edom, Moab, and the Ammonites;
all the kings of Tyre and Sidon; the kings of the coastlands across the sea;
Dedan, Tema, Buz, and all who cut the corners of their hair;
all the kings of Arabia, and all the kings of the mixed tribes who dwell in the desert;
all the kings of Zimri, Elam, and Media;
all the kings of the north, both near and far, one after another, all the kingdoms on the face of the earth. And after all of them, the king of Sheshach will drink it too.
“Then you are to tell them that this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘Drink, get drunk, and vomit. Fall down and never get up again, because of the sword I will send among you.’
If they refuse to take the cup from your hand and drink it, you are to tell them that this is what the LORD of Hosts says: ‘You most certainly must drink it!

For behold, I am beginning to bring disaster on the city that bears My Name, so how could you possibly go unpunished? You will not go unpunished, for I am calling down a sword upon all the inhabitants of the earth, declares the LORD of Hosts.’

units.label: The LORD's roar against all the earth

verses.text:
So you are to prophesy all these words against them and say to them: 
‘The LORD will roar from on high; He will raise His voice from His holy habitation. He will roar loudly over His pasture; like those who tread the grapes, He will call out with a shout against all the inhabitants of the earth.
The tumult will resound to the ends of the earth because the LORD brings a charge against the nations. He brings judgment on all mankind and puts the wicked to the sword,’” declares the LORD.

This is what the LORD of Hosts says: 
“Behold! Disaster is spreading from nation to nation; a mighty storm is rising from the ends of the earth.”
Those slain by the LORD on that day will be spread from one end of the earth to the other. They will not be mourned, gathered, or buried. They will be like dung lying on the ground.
Wail, you shepherds, and cry out; roll in the dust, you leaders of the flock. For the days of your slaughter have come; you will fall and be shattered like fine pottery.
Flight will evade the shepherds, and escape will elude the leaders of the flock.

Hear the cry of the shepherds, the wailing of the leaders of the flock, 
for the LORD is destroying their pasture.
The peaceful meadows have been silenced because of the LORD's burning anger.
He has left His den like a lion, for their land has been made a desolation by the sword of the oppressor, and because of the fierce anger of the LORD.

shivim.gloss: Seventy years, the fixed length God sets on Judah's exile under Babylon. Far from an open-ended punishment, it is a bounded discipline with a promised end, later confirmed as fulfilled in Daniel 9:2 and cited by Zechariah 1:12, showing God's judgments come with a measured limit, not limitless wrath.

kos.gloss: Cup, here the cup of the wine of God's wrath that every nation, starting with Jerusalem itself, must drink in turn. This image of a cup of judgment recurs throughout Scripture and finds its most solemn use in Christ's own agonized prayer in Gethsemane over the cup He was about to drink on the world's behalf.

basic.context: Jeremiah looks back over twenty-three years of faithfully warning Judah, only to be ignored again and again, and now announces that the coming Babylonian exile has a fixed length, seventy years. But the chapter does not stop with Judah; God hands Jeremiah a cup of wrath that every surrounding nation, and finally the whole earth, must drink, showing that Judah's judgment is part of a far larger reckoning God is bringing on all mankind.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage combines specific, datable prophecy, the seventy years, later confirmed exactly in Daniel 9:2, with a much broader universal scope, judgment on all mankind, verse 31. Readers should hold both together: God's word here is precise enough to be historically verified, yet its ultimate horizon is not Judah alone but every nation and every wicked deed on earth.
basic.originalLanguages: The exile is fixed at shivim shanah, seventy years, a real, countable discipline with a real end, not endless punishment. And the image running through the chapter's center is the kos, the cup, of God's wrath, first pressed to Judah's own lips before every nation on earth is required to drink from the same hand, an image later taken up in its most solemn form by Christ Himself in Gethsemane.
basic.typology: The cup of wrath that every nation must drink anticipates the ultimate cup of divine judgment, which Christ prays might pass from Him in Gethsemane, yet willingly drinks on behalf of all who trust in Him. The fixed seventy years, ending in restoration, anticipates the pattern of all divine discipline in Scripture, bounded, purposeful, and always aimed at eventual mercy for those who turn back.
basic.christ: Christ's agonized prayer in Gethsemane, 'let this cup pass from Me,' Matthew 26:39, draws directly on this very image of the cup of God's wrath that Jeremiah describes every nation as needing to drink; Christ instead drinks it fully on behalf of His people, so that those united to Him need not drink it themselves. The LORD's roar of judgment against all mankind here finds its answer in the gospel, where that judgment falls instead on Christ for all who receive Him.
basic.god: God is patient beyond what seems reasonable, sending prophet after prophet for twenty-three years before finally acting, yet He is also universally just, holding not only Judah but every nation, Egypt, Philistia, Edom, Moab, Babylon itself, accountable to the same standard of righteousness. His judgments, though severe, are also measured, fixed at seventy years rather than left open-ended, revealing mercy even within wrath.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 9:2 confirms Jeremiah's seventy years as a specific, fulfilled prophecy that Daniel himself studied and prayed over. Matthew 26:39 shows Christ praying over the same image of a cup of divine wrath, which He then drinks on behalf of sinners. Isaiah 51:17-22 uses the same cup imagery, later promising it will be taken from Jerusalem's hand and given instead to her tormentors. Revelation 14:10 describes final judgment using the identical image of the cup of God's wrath.
basic.oneStory: The cup of wrath that every nation must drink shows that Judah's judgment was never a private matter between God and one people; it previews a reckoning that touches all mankind. That universal judgment finds its resolution at the cross, where Christ drinks the cup of wrath in full so that everyone who trusts in Him is spared from having to drink it themselves.

context.literary: This chapter functions as a structural hinge in the book, closing the long section of oracles against Judah, chapters 1-25, before Jeremiah's ministry narrative and the oracles against foreign nations that follow later in the book, chapters 46-51, are anticipated here in miniature through the long list of nations who must drink the cup.
context.historical: Dated to 605 BC, the same year Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt at Carchemish and began his rise to regional dominance, this chapter marks the moment Babylon's supremacy over the whole region, not just Judah, became unmistakable, giving Jeremiah's twenty-three years of warning their historical vindication.
context.canonical: The seventy years prophecy becomes one of the most historically verified predictions in the Old Testament, explicitly cited and calculated by Daniel in Babylon, Daniel 9:2, and referenced again by Zechariah after the exile ended, Zechariah 1:12, anchoring the trustworthiness of Jeremiah's prophetic word.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To vindicate twenty-three years of ignored prophetic warning by announcing a specific, bounded judgment on Judah, while widening the scope to show that God's justice extends to every nation, not Judah alone, so that no people can consider themselves exempt from His moral accountability.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage records a specific historical prophecy with a specific, later-fulfilled timeframe; it is not prescribing a universal seventy-year pattern for every judgment. It does establish an enduring principle, that God's discipline, however severe, is bounded and purposeful, and that His justice is impartial across all nations.

debates.question: How should the seventy years be counted, and does it matter for the chapter's message?

views.label: From the first deportation, 605 BC, to the decree of Cyrus, 538 BC
views.case: This reading counts roughly seventy years of Babylonian dominance beginning with Daniel's own deportation, matching Daniel 9:2's application of the prophecy to his own generation nearing its end.

views.label: From the temple's destruction, 586 BC, to its rebuilding completion, 516 BC
views.case: This reading counts seventy years of the temple lying in ruins specifically, matching the concern of Zechariah 1:12 with mourning over Jerusalem and the temple during that same span.

debates.bearing: Either calculation lands within a real, verifiable historical window, and the chapter's main point, that God's judgment is bounded and will end in restoration, holds regardless of which starting point is preferred.

study.originalLanguages: The exile is fixed at shivim shanah, seventy years, real, calendared discipline rather than open-ended wrath, later confirmed by Daniel's own careful reading of this prophecy, Daniel 9:2. The chapter's central image is the kos, the cup of the wine of wrath, first placed in Judah's own hands before being passed to every nation on earth, an image Christ Himself would take up in His most agonized prayer.

typology.type: The cup of the LORD's wrath passed to every nation, Jeremiah 25:15-29
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the cup of divine wrath Christ prays over in Gethsemane and then drinks fully on behalf of His people, Matthew 26:39, so that all who trust Him are spared from drinking it themselves.

typology.type: The bounded seventy years of discipline, Jeremiah 25:11-12
typology.fulfillment: Models the pattern of all divine discipline in Scripture, painful yet purposeful and time-limited, always aimed at eventual restoration for those who turn back to God.

study.christ: The cup of God's wrath that every nation is commanded to drink in this chapter reappears at the very center of the gospel, when Christ, in Gethsemane, prays that this same cup might pass from Him, Matthew 26:39, yet goes on to drink it fully at the cross on behalf of all who would trust in Him. Where Jeremiah's cup brought staggering ruin to every nation that drank it, Christ's drinking of that cup brings, for His people, the exact opposite: forgiveness, peace, and life instead of judgment.
study.god: God's patience is extraordinary, sending prophet after prophet for twenty-three years before finally bringing the judgment He had long warned of, yet His justice is thoroughly impartial, holding every nation, not Judah alone, to account for its evil. Even His severest judgments carry a built-in limit, seventy years, revealing that His purposes in discipline are always aimed toward an eventual end and restoration, not endless destruction.

crossReferences.note: Daniel explicitly reads and calculates Jeremiah's seventy years prophecy, confirming it as a specific, historically anchored prediction.
Uses the same image of the cup of wrath, later promising it will be removed from Jerusalem's hand and given to her tormentors instead.
Christ prays over this same cup of divine wrath in Gethsemane before drinking it fully at the cross on behalf of sinners.
Describes final eschatological judgment using the identical image of the cup of God's wrath, poured out undiluted.

study.oneStory: The cup of wrath handed first to Jerusalem and then to every nation on earth shows that human sin and divine judgment are never merely a private, local matter, they touch the whole world. That universal reckoning finds its resolution in Christ, who drinks the cup of wrath completely at the cross so that everyone who trusts in Him is forever spared from having to drink it themselves.

quiz.q: How long had Jeremiah been prophesying to Judah before this chapter, according to verse 3?

options.text: Twenty-three years, from the thirteenth year of Josiah until this very day.
options.note: This detail underscores the sheer patience of God's warnings before judgment finally falls.

options.text: Only a few months.
options.note: The text specifies a much longer period of twenty-three years.

options.text: Seventy years, the same as the length of exile.
options.note: Seventy years is the length of the coming exile, not the length of Jeremiah's ministry up to this point.

options.text: Since before Jeremiah was born.
options.note: The twenty-three years is measured from Jeremiah's own call, not before his birth.

quiz.q: What is significant about the seventy years fixed for Judah's captivity?

options.text: It is a bounded, purposeful discipline with a definite end, later confirmed as historically fulfilled in Daniel 9:2.
options.note: The judgment is real and severe, yet limited rather than open-ended, showing mercy within justice.

options.text: It means the exile would never actually end.
options.note: The passage specifies a fixed term, after which Babylon itself would be judged and the exile would end.

options.text: It refers to seventy individual people taken into exile.
options.note: Seventy years is a length of time, not a count of individuals.

options.text: It was never actually fulfilled in later biblical history.
options.note: Daniel 9:2 shows this prophecy being read and applied as a real, fulfilled timeframe.

quiz.q: Who is commanded to drink the cup of the LORD's wrath in this chapter?

options.text: Jerusalem first, and then every surrounding nation and kingdom on the face of the earth.
options.note: Verses 18 to 26 list an extensive roster of nations, beginning with Jerusalem itself, all required to drink.

options.text: Only Babylon, with no other nation included.
options.note: The list explicitly includes many nations beyond Babylon, including Jerusalem itself first.

options.text: Only the nations who had never heard of the God of Israel.
options.note: The list includes Jerusalem and Judah, who certainly knew the LORD, showing knowledge of God does not exempt anyone from this judgment.

options.text: No one; the cup is only a metaphor with no actual recipients named.
options.note: The chapter names an extensive, specific list of nations required to drink the cup.

quiz.q: What is the scope of the judgment described in verses 30 to 31?

options.text: The LORD brings a charge against the nations and judgment on all mankind, not Judah alone.
options.note: Verse 31 explicitly states 'He brings judgment on all mankind,' a universal scope beyond any single nation.

options.text: It is limited strictly to the city of Jerusalem.
options.note: The passage explicitly widens the scope to 'all the inhabitants of the earth' and 'all mankind.'

options.text: It applies only to religious leaders, not ordinary people.
options.note: The judgment described is universal in scope, not limited to any one class of people.

options.text: It describes only a future judgment with no present application.
options.note: The judgment described is announced as imminent and historical, following directly from the twenty-three years of warning already given.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament pick up the image of the cup of wrath from this chapter?

options.text: Christ prays over this same cup of divine wrath in Gethsemane and then drinks it fully at the cross on behalf of His people.
options.note: Matthew 26:39 draws directly on this image, showing Christ bearing the judgment described here so believers do not have to.

options.text: The New Testament never references the image of a cup of wrath.
options.note: Matthew 26:39 and Revelation 14:10 both use this exact image directly.

options.text: It is used only to describe a literal cup used at the Last Supper with no connection to judgment.
options.note: While the Last Supper cup is a separate image, Christ's Gethsemane prayer specifically concerns the cup of wrath, not a literal drinking vessel from the meal.

options.text: It is applied only to unbelievers with no connection to Christ Himself.
options.note: The Gospels show Christ Himself drinking this cup of wrath on behalf of His people, not only describing it as applying to others.

questions: God sent prophet after prophet for twenty-three years before judgment fell. Is there a warning you have been hearing repeatedly that you have not yet heeded?
The seventy years of exile were painful but bounded, aimed at eventual restoration. How does knowing God's discipline has a purpose and an end change how you endure a hard season?
Every nation, not Judah alone, had to drink the cup of wrath; no one could claim exemption. Where might you be assuming you are exempt from accountability that actually applies to you too?
Christ drank the cup of wrath fully so His people would not have to. How does knowing that change the weight you carry over your own sin and guilt?
The LORD's judgment reaches 'all mankind', not one nation only. How does that shape the way you think about God's justice toward the whole world, not just your own circle?

translationNotes.note: 'Seventy years' is best understood as a round, symbolically complete number describing a full generation of Babylonian dominance, while also matching closely with the actual historical span later confirmed by Daniel.

'The cup of the wine of wrath' is a common prophetic image for inescapable divine judgment, appearing also in Isaiah, Habakkuk, and Revelation, always describing a reckoning no one can refuse to drink.

'Sheshach' is widely understood as a coded name for Babylon, using a Hebrew cipher called atbash; naming Babylon last and by a veiled name underscores that even Babylon itself, the instrument of judgment, would not escape drinking the same cup.
